title: Pledge

jokes.text: I love you, too, Tom. Go fuck yourself.
jokes.setup: Tom sincerely says 'I love you' to Selina as a warm farewell
jokes.punchline: Selina returns the sentiment and immediately pivots to 'Go fuck yourself' — the juxtaposition captures her entire character in one breath
jokes.explanation: A perfect cold open joke. The structure is flawless: the sincerity of 'I love you, too' sets up warmth, then 'Go fuck yourself' demolishes it instantly. Only Selina can deliver this. Maximum economy — five words after the comma do all the work. The joke distills her entire personality: she can feel genuine affection and contempt simultaneously without any cognitive dissonance. Scores 9+ on craft because the character integration is near-perfect and it's structurally elegant.

jokes.text: The only reason you were hired is because Mr. Tanz told me you were the best and because? Because of my court-ordered chemical castration.
jokes.setup: Someone is being questioned about their qualifications for a campaign hire
jokes.punchline: The second qualification is court-ordered chemical castration — an utterly absurd and dark credential delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and specific. The joke works because the setup implies a normal credentials conversation, and the punchline reveals a grotesque but politically logical reason (presumably around a female candidate). The deadpan delivery implied by the structure is what makes it land. Loses points on character integration since the characters aren't established enough yet.

jokes.text: Is chocolate bad for dogs? Oh no, not bad. Deadly. Okay.
jokes.setup: In the middle of a serious campaign conversation, Richard asks about chocolate and dogs
jokes.punchline: The correction from 'bad' to 'deadly' is delivered casually and Richard just says 'Okay' — implying he may have just realized he's about to kill a dog
jokes.explanation: Classic Richard — non sequitur concern delivered with complete guilelessness, followed by stoic acceptance of terrible news. The 'Okay' is the punchline. Perfect economy. Also seeds the dog mayor subplot.

jokes.text: I would like to schedule an appointment. It's for an abortion.
jokes.setup: Mundane campaign chatter is happening; Amy drops this clinical announcement mid-scene
jokes.punchline: The banality of phrasing ('schedule an appointment') followed by 'It's for an abortion' — as if booking a haircut
jokes.explanation: Amy's character is defined by ruthless pragmatism and suppressed emotion — this delivery is perfectly her. The bureaucratic framing of a deeply personal event is pure Iannucci. Economy is exceptional; the joke is exactly as long as it needs to be.

jokes.text: Richard, you can't keep working on both campaigns. But they're both equally good people.
jokes.setup: Richard is told he can't work for two opposing presidential campaigns simultaneously
jokes.punchline: His justification is that both candidates are 'equally good people' — a complete non-answer that sidesteps the obvious conflict of interest
jokes.explanation: Richard's defining characteristic — earnest obliviousness — on full display. He genuinely doesn't see the problem, which is funnier than any self-aware excuse. The joke is entirely in character.

jokes.text: It sounds like Dr. Seuss fucked Maya Angelou in the yuzz-ma-tuzz and then filled her all up with snoozily-scuzz.
jokes.setup: Kemi is doing call-and-response chants with a huge crowd, which is politically effective
jokes.punchline: Selina's contemptuous description mixes Dr. Seuss nonsense words with a sexual image — vicious, specific, and hilarious
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout lines. The invention of plausible-sounding Seussian nonsense words ('yuzz-ma-tuzz,' 'snoozily-scuzz') paired with sexual content is genuinely original — it requires creative effort to write. It captures Selina's cattiness, her racism-adjacent contempt, and her wit all at once. The commitment to the extended metaphor across two lines actually enhances it. Near-perfect character integration and originality.

jokes.text: How is this possibly even working for her? / Socio-politically, protest chants have proved an effective method-- / OK, off.
jokes.setup: Selina asks a rhetorical question in contempt of Kemi's chants
jokes.punchline: Kent begins a sincere academic explanation and Selina shuts it down immediately with 'OK, off' — dismissing both the answer and the person
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep rhythm: Kent's data-brain responds literally to a rhetorical question, and Selina's 'OK, off' is the perfect Selina dismissal. Works because it's character-specific and economical.

jokes.text: You know what else is huge, Amy? / Not even really showing.
jokes.setup: Amy notes Kemi's crowds are huge; Selina seems about to make a sexually crass joke about Amy's pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Gary innocently deflects by noting Amy's pregnancy isn't showing yet — both subverting the expected crassness and being accidentally cruel
jokes.explanation: The misdirection works on two levels: we expect Selina to make a crass pregnancy joke, but Gary interrupts with a non-sequitur that's both more innocent and somehow still uncomfortable. Selina's implied setup makes Gary's literal response funnier.

jokes.text: Selina's campaign slogan 'Time to finish the unfinished business of four years ago' being recited back incorrectly by the crowd, followed by her failed 'New Selina Now' correction
jokes.setup: Selina launches into her crowd participation moment with her campaign slogan
jokes.punchline: The crowd keeps adding 'of four years ago' when she doesn't want it, then they don't know 'New Selina Now' — her brand is completely failing in real time
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured cringe comedy. The slogan itself is self-undermining (admitting she failed), then the crowd makes it worse by embracing exactly the part she hates. The 'New Selina Now' failure is the final twist of the knife. Strong visual/performance comedy inferred from the transcript.

jokes.text: When I say 'shit,' you say 'show.'
jokes.setup: Selina's rally is in chaos; Ben watches from the sidelines
jokes.punchline: Ben's aside summarizing the whole disaster as a 'shit show' via a call-and-response format is perfectly timed nihilism
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and structure. Ben co-opts Kemi's successful call-and-response format to summarize Selina's disaster — it's a callback AND a character joke AND a situational gag all in eight words. Ben's world-weary voice is pitch-perfect for this. The meta-commentary on the scene is delivered in the exact form it's criticizing.

jokes.text: 'Kemi Fever.' Three exclamation points. / It's a pandemic.
jokes.setup: Time magazine's 'Kemi Fever' cover with three exclamation points
jokes.punchline: Kent's dry 'It's a pandemic' applies epidemiological framing to a political trend with zero affect
jokes.explanation: Kent's literalism applied to metaphor is a reliable character gag. 'Pandemic' is perfectly chosen — clinical, definitive, and bleak. The two-word punchline is maximum economy.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, somebody sneezed on our campaign, now we're bleeding out our assholes.
jokes.setup: The pandemic/fever metaphor established by Kent
jokes.punchline: Selina extends the illness metaphor to its most grotesque possible endpoint — 'bleeding out our assholes'
jokes.explanation: Selina's gift for taking a metaphor and driving it off a cliff is on full display. 'Bleeding out our assholes' is specific, grotesque, and characteristically her — she cannot resist escalation. Good callback structure.

jokes.text: Last thing I need is my picture being taken eating dick-shaped food. I'd rather eat a food-shaped dick.
jokes.setup: The team suggests Selina eat corn dogs at the county fair for retail politics
jokes.punchline: Selina's objection to the phallic optics, then the pivot to 'food-shaped dick' as the supposedly better alternative — absurd but weirdly logical in her mind
jokes.explanation: The inversion — 'food-shaped dick' as the preferable alternative — is clever and characteristically Selina. The logic is impeccable within her vanity. The line is quotable and punchy. Loses some originality points because the corn dog/phallic shape observation is well-worn territory, but the specific twist saves it.

jokes.text: Maybe somebody should be interviewing the 'Times' about why they write so much about modern dance.
jokes.setup: Concern that the NYT is profiling Keith Quinn as the real power behind the campaign
jokes.punchline: Selina deflects with a non-sequitur grievance about modern dance coverage — petulant, specific, and somehow plausible as a real Selina fixation
jokes.explanation: The absurdist specificity of 'modern dance' is what makes this work — it's not just a random deflection, it feels like a genuine elite cultural grievance Selina would actually have. Character-specific absurdism.

jokes.text: Thirty-six hours in Snoozeville. / That was my major.
jokes.setup: Selina dismisses Waterloo as 'Snoozeville'
jokes.punchline: Richard reveals he majored in 'Snoozeville' — treating her insult as a real academic subject with genuine pride
jokes.explanation: Richard's literal interpretation of idiom is a reliable and character-specific joke engine. 'That was my major' is perfectly timed and delivered — it redirects the insult as unintentional autobiography.

jokes.text: How are we going to RU486 Kemi's campaign? / No offense, Amy.
jokes.setup: Selina asks how to terminate Kemi's campaign
jokes.punchline: Using an abortion drug as a verb to mean 'destroy,' followed by 'No offense, Amy' — acknowledging Amy's abortion with weaponized casual cruelty
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The linguistic invention of using RU486 as a verb is genuinely original and requires knowledge to land. The callback to Amy's announcement is perfectly timed and the 'No offense, Amy' is Selina at her most vicious — she knows exactly what she's doing. The economy is superb: maximum darkness in minimum words. Justifies 8.5+ on all criteria.

jokes.text: Kemi is super impressive. And I'm not gonna lie, ma'am, very much my type.
jokes.setup: The team is discussing Kemi's political threat
jokes.punchline: Marjorie's robotic affect makes her casually revealing that Kemi is her romantic type all the more jarring and funny
jokes.explanation: Marjorie's deadpan is a reliable comedy engine. The mismatch between her affect and the personal revelation is the joke. The follow-up 'What's happening?' from someone in the room sells it further.

jokes.text: My God, pick a lane. Jesus.
jokes.setup: Marjorie reveals Kemi is her type, and Catherine defends strong women
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to what seems to be commentary on Catherine's bisexuality/fluid interests — 'pick a lane' is both homophobic in Selina's voice and absurd as a response
jokes.explanation: A functional character joke — Selina's casual homophobia/impatience is consistent. Not particularly original but economical and characterful.

jokes.text: A good union or, like, teachers?
jokes.setup: Dan reports Kemi picked up a union endorsement
jokes.punchline: Selina's instinctive class/political snobbery expressed as a genuine question — teachers' unions are not 'good' unions to her
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — six words revealing Selina's entire class worldview. The joke works because it's a genuine question from her perspective, not a conscious insult. That sincerity is what makes it sharp.

jokes.text: Actually, my uncle was a shop steward in the 7-4. Asbestos killed him. / Oh, no! / I'm sorry, Richard. / Asbestos was the name of their pit bull. It was a rescue that killed its first owner. That's why you have to go to breeders.
jokes.setup: Richard shares that asbestos killed his uncle, generating genuine sympathy
jokes.punchline: Asbestos was the dog's name; then a second punchline reveals the rescue dog killed its previous owner; then a third beat adds the breeders moral
jokes.explanation: A triple-escalation misdirection that keeps paying off. The initial misdirection (asbestos = dog name) is good; the reveal that the dog killed its first owner escalates the darkness; the anti-rescue dog moral is the perfect absurdist button. Three distinct laugh beats in rapid succession, each building on the last. Richard's delivery of devastating information as cheerful anecdote is perfectly character-integrated.

jokes.text: Fantastic. Her Achilles cock.
jokes.setup: The Andrew Meyer scandal is identified as Selina's campaign weakness — her Achilles heel
jokes.punchline: 'Achilles cock' replaces 'heel' with a vulgar term, reflecting the Andrew/sexual/infidelity dimension of the problem
jokes.explanation: Clean wordplay that does double duty — anatomical pun on 'heel' while also capturing the Andrew scandal's sexual/marital dimension. Very economical. Dan's breezy vulgarity suits it perfectly.

jokes.text: Andrew: Monnie and I are still together. Better than ever, in fact. / No. Felicia is my... paralegal.
jokes.setup: Selina sees Andrew with a woman named Felicia and asks about his girlfriend Monnie
jokes.punchline: Andrew's 'Better than ever' immediately undercut by him having to clarify Felicia is just his 'paralegal' — delivered with a telling pause
jokes.explanation: Andrew's compulsive infidelity and transparent lying is a reliable source of comedy. The pause before 'paralegal' does the work. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Oh, God, we're all goin' to jail.
jokes.setup: Andrew's 'paralegal' Felicia apparently knows about the Meyer Fund and Andrew is about to try to access the ledgers
jokes.punchline: Selina's calm, resigned acceptance that they're going to prison
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — Selina's resignation is funny, but the joke is fairly predictable given the context.

jokes.text: Can you find me a real green juice somewhere in Iowa? I'm drinking Odwalla like some country lesbian who just got to the big city.
jokes.setup: Selina is at a county fair in Iowa, out of her element
jokes.punchline: The specific image of 'country lesbian who just got to the big city' as her metaphor for drinking Odwalla is absurdly specific and contemptuous
jokes.explanation: Selina's class contempt filtered through an unexpectedly vivid and specific image. The country lesbian detail is both specific enough to be funny and characteristically Selina in its condescension. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Happy Abortion Eve.
jokes.setup: Dan encounters Amy and others the evening before Amy's abortion
jokes.punchline: Treating the abortion like a holiday — 'Abortion Eve' like 'Christmas Eve' — with cheerful delivery
jokes.explanation: Three words. Maximum economy, maximum darkness, maximum character specificity (only Dan would say this with this particular mixture of genuine good cheer and total emotional vacancy). 'Abortion Eve' as a holiday framing is highly original and quotable. One of the episode's most memorable lines. Justifies 8.5 craft.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! / What? Don't they all know?
jokes.setup: Dan says 'Happy Abortion Eve' in front of a group
jokes.punchline: Dan's genuine confusion about why Amy is horrified — he sees nothing wrong with announcing this publicly
jokes.explanation: The follow-up to 'Abortion Eve' — Dan's obliviousness extends the joke and his 'Don't they all know?' reveals just how emotionally tone-deaf he is about Amy's situation. The reaction beat sells it.

jokes.text: OK, I don't know if I should be sad or turned on.
jokes.setup: Ben witnesses Dan and Amy's toxic dynamic around the abortion
jokes.punchline: Ben's admission that he can't distinguish arousal from sympathy in response to this situation is both self-aware and disturbing
jokes.explanation: Ben's deadpan confession is a good character beat — his emotional dysregulation is a running character trait. The economy is strong and the 'Eww' response from someone else perfectly punctuates it.

jokes.text: Actually, I tried to quit last week, and the president gave me a raise. / So quit Jonah. / He gave me a raise too.
jokes.setup: Richard is told he should quit one of the two campaigns he's working on
jokes.punchline: Both candidates gave him raises when he tried to quit — he's trapped by mutual incompetence on both sides
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure — the second raise mirrors the first, and the implication that both candidates desperately need Richard while he's the most capable person in the room is a nice running commentary on the field. Clean two-beat structure.

jokes.text: Jonah Ryan's campaign ad: punching senators, kicking female candidates, 'Undisputed President of the United States!' followed by 'I'm Jonah Ryan, and I wanna suck this message's hot clam.'
jokes.setup: Jonah's campaign team screens his new political ad
jokes.punchline: The ad escalates from standard aggressive political posturing to Jonah kicking a woman in the vagina to the tagline 'I wanna suck this message's hot clam' — a grotesque corruption of the standard 'I approve this message'
jokes.explanation: The political ad parody is multi-layered: it starts as typical aggressive politicking and escalates to pure Jonah id. The corruption of 'I approve this message' into 'I wanna suck this message's hot clam' is genuinely original and perfectly character-integrated — only Jonah. The team member's 'Me too!' is a great button. High craft for combining political satire with character comedy.

jokes.text: I ad-libbed. / Teddy, improv is Jonah's thing. You should've heard his wedding vows. He did a really funny Chinese voice. / Yeah, I expressed how horny I was.
jokes.setup: Jonah went off-script in his campaign ad
jokes.punchline: Three escalating reveals: improv is his 'thing'; he did a Chinese voice at his wedding; he expressed horniness in his vows
jokes.explanation: Another triple-escalation from a single setup. 'He did a really funny Chinese voice' at his wedding is appalling in the best Jonah way; 'I expressed how horny I was' as his summary of his own wedding vows is perfect character comedy. Each revelation is worse than the last, and Jonah delivers the final line with genuine pride.

jokes.text: Most people are uncomfortable watching a white man kick a black woman in the vagina. / Well, I don't see vagina color.
jokes.setup: Focus testing showed the campaign ad's physical violence against Kemi was unpopular
jokes.punchline: Jonah's 'I don't see vagina color' — a parody of 'I don't see color' colorblindness rhetoric applied to genitals with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The satire of colorblindness rhetoric applied to genitalia is original, pointed, and perfectly Jonah — he genuinely believes this is a coherent defense. The economy is near-perfect. The joke works on multiple levels: satirizing both his racism and the hollow 'I don't see color' deflection. Justifies 8.5 craft score.

jokes.text: And, good news-- No, that's bad news. / I'm at less than 5%? / In the plus column, the undercard debate will be first, which means we have no problem getting out of the parking lot. / Oh, no, that's bad news too. It's stacked parking.
jokes.setup: Jonah's campaign is told he's in the undercard debate for polling under 5%
jokes.punchline: The staffer tries to find a silver lining (easier parking egress), but it turns out it's stacked parking — every attempted positive is immediately a negative
jokes.explanation: Well-structured triple reversal — good news/bad news/silver lining/also bad. The specificity of 'stacked parking' as the final defeat is absurdly mundane and that's what makes it land. Good structural comedy.

jokes.text: Andrew, you said there was nothing illegal about the fund. / I thought we both understood I was lying.
jokes.setup: Selina confronts Andrew about the Meyer Fund
jokes.punchline: Andrew's breezy assumption that they both knew he was lying all along — stated as a simple misunderstanding about mutual awareness
jokes.explanation: 'I thought we both understood I was lying' is a genuinely brilliant line — it reframes the lie as a shared social contract, implying Selina is naive for not having internalized this. The casual certainty of delivery is key. Exceptional economy and structure. This line could function as a show tagline.

jokes.text: Yeah. 'Don't worry, Lee. You won't get pregnant. I'll pull out and cum on your back.' Again, I thought we both understood I was lying.
jokes.setup: Andrew tells Lee 'Don't worry, I'll keep you out of it' regarding the fund fraud
jokes.punchline: Lee translates Andrew's promise using a sexual metaphor that mirrors his actual behavior, and Andrew confirms he was lying in the same language as before
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'I thought we both understood I was lying' gives the structure extra resonance — it's now established as Andrew's personal philosophy. Lee's sexual translation of the professional lie is crude but structurally elegant. The callback payoff is satisfying.

jokes.text: And since your tenure was so unusually brief, I took the liberty of using President Ford's ceremony as a template.
jokes.setup: A general is presenting funeral plans for Selina
jokes.punchline: Using Ford — the least historically significant recent president — as the template, delivered as a technical matter-of-fact
jokes.explanation: The Ford reference is well-chosen — he's the historically forgotten accidental president, which mirrors Selina's brief tenure perfectly. Delivered as bureaucratic common sense, which makes the insult land harder.

jokes.text: Who? / Uh, that's not gonna work. He was lucky not to get dumped out of a pickup truck into a quarry.
jokes.setup: General says Ford will be the template
jokes.punchline: Gary not knowing who Ford is, followed by Selina's grotesque description of what Ford deserved
jokes.explanation: Two quick beats: Gary's ignorance (character) and Selina's extreme contempt for Ford (character). 'Dumped out of a pickup truck into a quarry' is characteristically graphic Selina. Efficient double joke.

jokes.text: My vision for when I die... / If you... / No, I am gonna die. / Mm... / More of a sort of a Princess Diana thing. But classy. But no Prince Charles. / Oh, yeah. He's worse than the mother.
jokes.setup: Selina is planning her own funeral with the general
jokes.punchline: The Diana comparison (aspirational), then 'but classy' (self-aware), then the exclusion of Charles, then Gary's evaluation that Charles is 'worse than the mother' — Diana's death was caused in part by fleeing paparazzi, making this casually dark
jokes.explanation: Gary's unexpected strong opinion about the royals ('worse than the mother') is a nice character beat. Selina's 'but classy' as a qualifier to Diana is good self-awareness comedy. Sustained sequence of small beats.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. [re: Prince Charles] He's worse than the mother.
jokes.setup: Selina says she wants her funeral like Diana's but 'no Prince Charles'
jokes.punchline: Gary enthusiastically agrees and elevates it — Charles is 'worse than the mother,' meaning worse than the Queen, who is a fairly significant figure to be worse than
jokes.explanation: A functional follow-on beat. Gary's royal gossip as a character beat is fine but not elevated.

jokes.text: Ma'am, why don't we just jump right to embalming options to minimize decomp during the rotunda viewing.
jokes.setup: Selina is excited about planning her funeral and the general is trying to be practical
jokes.punchline: The general's clinical pivot to decomp prevention as an urgent practical matter, bypassing all the ceremonial discussion
jokes.explanation: 'Decomp during the rotunda viewing' delivered as a standard logistical concern is a great tonal mismatch. The bureaucratic horror of treating Selina's body as a preservation problem is darkly funny.

jokes.text: Jonah is doing physical comedy with food at the fair — 'It's shitting in my mouth' / 'He's pretending the potato is pooping in his mouth.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is at the county fair trying to endear himself to voters
jokes.punchline: Jonah holds a potato near his face making it 'poop' in his mouth; Teddy has to explain to the reporter what's happening
jokes.explanation: The visual gag of Teddy having to narrate Jonah's childish behavior to a reporter is funnier than the bit itself. 'He's pretending the potato is pooping in his mouth' as a straight-faced explanation captures the indignity of managing Jonah perfectly.

jokes.text: Well, ever since I got it, they stopped calling me 'Old Guy.' Now I'm 'Hat Guy.' / It's 'Fat Guy.' / Hey, Mike, have you given any thought to that thing that we talked about?
jokes.setup: Mike explains his hat has changed his newsroom nickname
jokes.punchline: Wendy casually corrects that it was always 'Fat Guy' not 'Old Guy,' then immediately pivots to asking about adopting a baby — the correction barely registers
jokes.explanation: Good Mike comedy — his obliviousness to his own nickname combined with the casual cruelty of the correction by his own wife. The immediate pivot to baby adoption amplifies the domestically brutal dynamic.

jokes.text: Somehow they got the idea I'm a good writer. And I'm not!
jokes.setup: Mike explains his pressure at BuzzFeed due to the Felix Wade piece
jokes.punchline: Mike's emphatic, self-aware declaration that he is not a good writer — as if this is the main problem with the situation
jokes.explanation: Mike's consistent self-awareness about his own incompetence, delivered with distress, is a reliable character joke. The delivery — 'And I'm not!' — is the key; the exclamation point suggests genuine anguish.

jokes.text: I didn't use to be Hat Guy?
jokes.setup: Lizzie calls Mike 'Fat Guy' confirming Wendy's correction
jokes.punchline: Mike's plaintive question reveals he genuinely believed the hat had rebranded him
jokes.explanation: Good button to the hat gag — Mike's hope crushed by a third source. Functional, not exceptional.

jokes.text: Besides, if I was, you'd know. I'd have retard face or whatever.
jokes.setup: Teddy suggests Jonah might be intellectually disabled
jokes.punchline: Jonah's self-exculpation: he'd be visibly identifiable if he were ('retard face'), which he states as factual and reassuring
jokes.explanation: The casual confidence of 'retard face or whatever' — Jonah's breezy anatomical certainty — is character-perfect. He's not trying to be offensive; he genuinely believes this is a helpful clarification. The 'or whatever' is a nice touch of studied indifference.

jokes.text: They don't know what that word is. / Good point.
jokes.setup: Selina starts to say 'caucus' to the crowd
jokes.punchline: Kent notes the Iowa crowd may not know the term; Selina immediately agrees without embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire compressed into three words of exchange. The condescension toward Iowa voters is double-edged — it mocks both the politicians and implies the voters are ignorant. Good economy.

jokes.text: That's a busy beaver.
jokes.setup: A woman at the fair confirms all the many children with her are hers
jokes.punchline: 'Busy beaver' — ostensibly commenting on the mother's busy life but with an obvious anatomical double meaning delivered with Selina's smile
jokes.explanation: The double entendre is obvious and the beaver pun is well-worn. What saves it slightly is Selina's delivery context — she may or may not mean it, which is characteristically her. But the joke itself is generic.

jokes.text: The current version only asks for donations and, for some reason, deletes your address book. Technically, it's more of a virus. I had it but had to delete it because it was making my phone hot.
jokes.setup: Kemi has a campaign app; does Selina?
jokes.punchline: Three-beat escalation: it only asks for donations → also deletes your address book → 'technically more of a virus' → Gary personally experienced the heating
jokes.explanation: The campaign app being 'more of a virus' is a good escalation joke with three distinct beats. Gary having personally used and suffered from Selina's own app is a nice absurdist detail. Clean structure.

jokes.text: Of course we do, ma'am. The current version only asks for donations and, for some reason, deletes your address book. Technically, it's more of a virus.
jokes.setup: Selina asks if they have a campaign app like Kemi's
jokes.punchline: The app is a virus — it only takes money and destroys contacts, which is essentially an accurate description of the Meyer campaign as a whole
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'for some reason, deletes your address book' to 'technically, it's more of a virus' is well-structured. The metaphorical resonance — the Meyer campaign is also more of a virus — elevates this beyond a simple tech joke. 'For some reason' is a perfect throwaway qualifier.

jokes.text: Perhaps that kind of thing plays well in 'Nevahda' -- / Nevada.
jokes.setup: Selina is dismissing Governor Calhoun's attacks
jokes.punchline: Selina mispronounces 'Nevada' as 'Nevahda' — and is corrected by Mike, of all people
jokes.explanation: The irony of Mike — the campaign's most incompetent member — correcting Selina on basic geography is good. The mispronunciation itself is a realistic presidential gaffe. Character inversion sells it.

jokes.text: Wow, Mike, you said an actual thing. How the fuck did that happen? It's like 'Flowers for Algernon.' / I understand that reference now. It's a book.
jokes.setup: Mike accidentally prompts Selina's 'non-negative pledge' with a real question
jokes.punchline: Selina's backhanded compliment invoking Flowers for Algernon (a story about temporary intelligence), followed by Mike's proud note that he now knows the reference — confirming he had previously been too stupid to get it
jokes.explanation: The Flowers for Algernon reference is precisely calibrated — it implies Mike's intelligence is temporary and borrowed. But the real genius is Mike's response: 'I understand that reference now. It's a book.' He's proving Selina's point by not fully understanding the insult. Double-layered character comedy with excellent structure.

jokes.text: Um... mentally retarded? / No, that-- that's wrong. / I see where your head is at, though, and you are technically correct.
jokes.setup: Sensitivity trainers ask Jonah if there's another word he could have used instead of 'retarded' — 'there are no wrong answers'
jokes.punchline: Jonah says 'mentally retarded' — technically more clinical but obviously still wrong; the trainer's anguished concession that he's 'technically correct' while also 'wrong' is perfectly calibrated
jokes.explanation: Jonah's response is perfectly in-character: he cannot comprehend why 'mentally retarded' is worse than 'retarded,' and technically he's correct (it's the clinical term). The trainer's anguished 'you are technically correct' followed by 'but that's wrong' captures the exact tonal impossibility of trying to educate Jonah. Character-specific, structurally excellent.

jokes.text: Oh, God, is that like math? / Actually, it's just a way of using empathy to help avoid hurting other people's feelings. / That's so gay.
jokes.setup: The sensitivity trainer introduces '360 degree perspective-taking'
jokes.punchline: Jonah's fear of it being math, then his 'That's so gay' response to an explanation about empathy — immediately using another slur to describe the concept of avoiding slurs
jokes.explanation: Structurally perfect — the very act of teaching Jonah not to use slurs triggers him using a different slur. The irony is airtight and completely character-integrated. 'Is that like math?' is also a great beat showing his fear of cognitive effort. The trainers' reactions sell it completely.

jokes.text: I didn't mean like gay gay or like 'This guy and his husband are gay.' I meant gay like, uh, mentally retarded.
jokes.setup: The trainer reacts in horror to 'That's so gay'
jokes.punchline: Jonah's clarification makes it worse in two directions simultaneously — distinguishing 'gay gay' from his usage, then defining his usage as 'mentally retarded' (bringing back the previous slur)
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of Jonah's circular logic. His attempt to clarify loops back to the previous slur he was being corrected for, creating a perfect closed loop of insensitivity. The distinction between 'gay gay' and 'this guy and his husband are gay' vs. 'mentally retarded' as his usage is both appalling and structurally brilliant. The callback to 'mentally retarded' gives it extra punch.

jokes.text: Is this guy on his fuckin' period? What is--
jokes.setup: The sensitivity trainer steps out to compose himself after Jonah's remarks
jokes.punchline: Jonah uses a sexist phrase to describe male distress, unaware of the irony given the context of his sensitivity training
jokes.explanation: A third slur-type remark from Jonah, but it's the weakest of the trio — the 'on his period' gag is less original than the previous beats. Still works in context but feels like the scene running slightly long.

jokes.text: My dumb slit sister was supposed to be my abortion ride, but now, of course, she is nowhere to be found, so I'm-- / Oh. That makes sense. / Dan: Yeah. / Well, would you mind? / Unless you plan on fucking her again while she's passed out. She doesn't call that rape, by the way, that's just regular sex to her.
jokes.setup: Amy needs a ride to her abortion; her sister bailed
jokes.punchline: Amy asks Dan — her ex — then preemptively addresses the elephant in the room about their toxic sexual history in graphic terms
jokes.explanation: The Amy/Dan dynamic is one of the show's most reliably dark wells. The line about 'fucking her again while she's passed out' being 'just regular sex to her' is both deeply uncomfortable and character-true. Dan's 'I will get my keys' after this speech is a great reaction beat.

jokes.text: She aborted her unborn fetus. — Dock her a day's pay then.
jokes.setup: Selina asks where Amy is
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to Amy's abortion is purely HR: dock her pay
jokes.explanation: Perfect Selina joke. The 'unborn fetus' construction by Richard (tautological and faintly absurd) is a setup beat, but 'Dock her a day's pay then' is exceptional — it takes a significant personal event and filters it entirely through Selina's managerial self-interest. This is who Selina Meyer is. The economy is extraordinary. Earns 8.6 comfortably.

jokes.text: Don't wear white pants!
jokes.setup: Dan is about to take Amy to get an abortion
jokes.punchline: Sophie's practical warning about white pants on abortion day — clinical and grotesque in its casualness
jokes.explanation: Three words, complete economy. The humor is in the casualness — treating the post-procedure as a normal practical concern, the way you'd warn someone about a muddy hike. Sophie's character is established entirely through this one line.

jokes.text: She is an actual murderer?! Yes! Who drives an import!
jokes.setup: Selina learns Kemi accidentally killed her boyfriend with a car at 16
jokes.punchline: After the shocking murder reveal, Selina's outrage pivots from the death itself to the fact that it was a Volkswagen Cabriolet — a foreign import — as if that's equally damning
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best jokes. The structure is perfect: appropriate outrage (murder), then the pivot to import car as a co-equal grievance. The deflation from murder to import preference is a masterclass in Selina's priorities — she cares more about the car brand than the death. Only Selina would make this jump. The exclamation points in the writing perfectly capture the escalating absurdity. Justifies near-9 across the board.

jokes.text: Where is that Fatty McFatty hiding today? She aborted her unborn fetus. [beat]
jokes.setup: Selina uses a fat-shaming nickname for Amy then immediately gets the abortion news
jokes.punchline: The tonal whiplash — petty fat-shaming immediately followed by 'aborted her unborn fetus' — with Gary's beat of silence between them
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of the petty nickname and the serious news is a tonal comedy beat. Works mainly as setup for the 'dock her pay' joke that follows.

jokes.text: These are clearly made with child labor. — Hold this. — I was an agricultural advisor. Nothing more.
jokes.setup: Someone at the fair is buying/using something (likely agricultural products or crafts)
jokes.punchline: Someone defensively insists they were 'just an agricultural advisor, nothing more' — the specificity and defensiveness suggests a darker history
jokes.explanation: A background gag — someone in the scene has a suspicious past involvement in agricultural settings (likely Vietnam/Latin America reference). Amusing but context-dependent and easily missed.

jokes.text: Keep your head down, Ames. I'll part the redneck sea.
jokes.setup: Anti-abortion protesters are outside the clinic
jokes.punchline: Dan frames himself as Moses parting the sea — but it's 'the redneck sea' — combining biblical grandiosity with casual contempt for rural protesters
jokes.explanation: Good Dan line — the Moses metaphor applied to clinic protesters has the right mix of self-aggrandizement and contempt that defines his character. 'Redneck sea' is economical and specific.

jokes.text: We gotta spread this news like Kemi's boyfriend's guts all over the pavement! / Actually, he was decapitated.
jokes.setup: Selina is excited to use the car accident story against Kemi
jokes.punchline: Selina uses a visceral spreading metaphor, then is corrected that it wasn't actually guts (decapitation) — the correction is somehow worse
jokes.explanation: The correction 'actually, he was decapitated' — delivered matter-of-factly to correct Selina's anatomically incorrect gore metaphor — is darkly excellent. The specificity of 'decapitated' is worse than 'guts on pavement' somehow, and the character who knows this detail and offers it as a clarification is a perfect Veep beat.

jokes.text: Well, why did you morons let me do that?
jokes.setup: Selina is told she can't use the murder story because social media praised her non-negative pledge
jokes.punchline: Selina blames her team for her own decision to take the pledge — her total lack of self-reflection delivered as righteous anger
jokes.explanation: A reliable Selina character beat — she never takes responsibility. The specific phrasing 'why did you morons let me do that' is perfectly her: the blame is external, the framing is that the team failed to stop her from her own idea. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Is there any way we can accuse Kemi of murdering her boyfriend, but in a positive way?
jokes.setup: Selina is bound by her non-negative pledge but wants to use the murder story
jokes.punchline: The logical impossibility of 'positively' accusing someone of murder — Selina genuinely expects her team to solve this
jokes.explanation: A superb line that crystallizes Selina's delusional political thinking. 'Accuse Kemi of murdering her boyfriend, but in a positive way' is the perfect synthesis of her two problems, asked as a genuine question expecting a solution. Perfectly economical, deeply character-specific, and highly quotable. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Selina approached by an unknown person at the fair who seems to grab or startle her — 'What was that? Where's Secret Service?'
jokes.setup: A fair worker approaches Selina with a 'guess your age/weight' offer
jokes.punchline: The startled reaction from a former president who should be well-protected — the mundane fair game is treated as a security incident
jokes.explanation: A quick physical beat — funny mainly as a visual (the fair worker vs. ex-president dignity gap). Not much craft but serviceable transition comedy.

jokes.text: Madam President! Guess your age? Guess your weight?
jokes.setup: Selina is walking through the fair in her presidential bubble
jokes.punchline: A fair barker immediately offers Selina the most age/weight sensitive game possible — right after she's been talking about image and optics
jokes.explanation: A good contextual beat — Selina's reaction to this specific fair game offer (after all the optics discussion) is implied. The timing is good. Not deeply crafted but earns its laugh from situational irony.

jokes.text: Yep, 10-4. Body's a wonderland. / Can I just sign the consent form?
jokes.setup: The clinic doctor lists surgical risks: infection, bleeding, uterine perforation
jokes.punchline: Amy responds to a list of serious medical risks with 'Body's a wonderland' — dismissive, darkly funny — then immediately asks for the consent form
jokes.explanation: 'Body's a wonderland' as a response to abortion risk warnings is both a John Mayer reference and a dismissal that captures Amy's armor-plated emotional style. The immediate 'Can I just sign the consent form?' confirms she's in pure efficiency mode. Very Amy.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah? You got any of those darts they use on rampaging circus animals?
jokes.setup: The nurse says she'll give Amy something to help her relax
jokes.punchline: Amy requests tranquilizer darts for circus animals as her preferred method of relaxation — implies she has the energy of a rampaging elephant
jokes.explanation: The circus animal dart request is a vivid and original metaphor for Amy's hypervigilance. It works on two levels: self-deprecating (she's a rampaging animal) and character-illuminating (she's never not on edge). Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Less talk, more abortion. — Patient has declined. — I am as sure... as I will be.
jokes.setup: The nurse is going through legally required Iowa pre-procedure counseling, offering ultrasound viewing
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'less talk, more abortion' dismisses Iowa's mandatory counseling with maximum impatience; her existential hedge 'I am as sure as I will be' is a beautifully human dark joke
jokes.explanation: 'Less talk, more abortion' is exceptional Amy — the bureaucratic process is friction to her, and she treats it like a restaurant order taking too long. 'I am as sure as I will be' is the best line in the sequence: it's existentially honest, darkly funny, and deeply Amy. Both lines would only work coming from this character in this moment. The nurse's flat 'Patient has declined' filing of Amy's emotional hedge as a clinical notation is also quietly excellent.

jokes.text: Is that the Berkeley VC-10? That's like the Shelby Cobra GT of vag-vacs.
jokes.setup: Dan accompanies Amy to her abortion; he notices the medical equipment
jokes.punchline: Dan evaluates the abortion vacuum with car-enthusiast energy — 'Shelby Cobra GT of vag-vacs' — treating it like a piece of impressive machinery
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The invented product name 'Berkeley VC-10' sounds plausibly real, and 'Shelby Cobra GT of vag-vacs' is a coinage of genuine invention. Only Dan — a sociopathic car-bro — would assess medical equipment with consumer enthusiasm. The nurse's reaction ('I'm sorry, who is this man?') perfectly punctuates it. High originality and character integration justify 8.5.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm the proud father-to-not-be.
jokes.setup: The nurse asks who Dan is after his vacuum-enthusiast commentary
jokes.punchline: 'Father-to-not-be' — a perversion of the expectant parent phrase, delivered with chest-puffing pride
jokes.explanation: 'Father-to-not-be' is a genuinely brilliant coinage — economical, dark, and only Dan would wear this as a badge of pride. The inversion of 'father-to-be' with 'not-be' is structurally elegant. One of the most quotable lines in the episode and perfectly character-integrated. Justifies 8.5+ craft.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, who is this man? — That's Dan. — Yeah, I'm the proud father-to-not-be.
jokes.setup: The nurse is alarmed that a man is in the procedure room analyzing the equipment
jokes.punchline: Dan introduces himself as 'the proud father-to-not-be' — a clever inversion of the standard proud father phrase
jokes.explanation: 'Father-to-not-be' is a perfect Veep construction — the standard phrase inverted to be precisely accurate to Dan's situation and his character (he wants full credit while avoiding all consequences). Highly quotable, excellent economy.

jokes.text: What is up with the Clubfoot Cunt-tessa?
jokes.setup: Selina asks about Kemi's political behavior at the debate
jokes.punchline: 'Clubfoot Cunt-tessa' — a portmanteau/insult nickname that combines a physical slur with a vaguely Italian title
jokes.explanation: Selina's nickname coinage is reliably inventive. 'Cunt-tessa' as a portmanteau of 'cunt' and 'contessa' is linguistically creative even as it's vicious. The 'clubfoot' addition is characteristically gratuitous.

jokes.text: What is up with the Clubfoot Cunt-tessa? I think she's worried that you might beat her up in the girls' bathroom, give her a swirly. Oh, no. Yeah, you would've. OK.
jokes.setup: Selina is asking about a rival candidate (implied to be Kemi) who seems intimidated by Selina
jokes.punchline: The 'swirly' suggestion prompts Selina's denial followed immediately by 'yeah, you would've' — a perfectly honest acknowledgment of Selina's brutality
jokes.explanation: 'Clubfoot Cunt-tessa' is the kind of Selina insult that's cruel but specific. The exchange about the swirly works because the 'Oh, no. Yeah, you would've.' correction comes from someone who knows Selina well enough to immediately revise the assessment.

jokes.text: Fresh-squeezed Billy Carter juicy.
jokes.setup: Selina teases Tom James about having 'something juicy' on Kemi
jokes.punchline: 'Billy Carter juicy' — invoking Jimmy Carter's embarrassing brother as a unit of measurement for political scandal
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Billy Carter as a unit of political embarrassment is excellent — specific enough to be funny for those who know it, the right era for Selina's cultural reference range. 'Fresh-squeezed' adds juice to the metaphor. Economical and surprisingly specific.

jokes.text: No. Forget it. This never works. / What am I thinking? / No, no, no, no. / Selina, listen, you got no reason to trust me, based on... / Yeah. Trying to screw me out of the presidency, then trying to actually screw me, then screwing me, then writing a book about screwing me. / Also discussed it on television. / Lovely.
jokes.setup: Tom James proposes a political alliance with Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina lists the ways Tom has wronged her — political screwing, actual screwing, screwing again, book, TV discussion — each 'screwing' landing as both literal and metaphorical; Tom's addition of 'television' and Selina's 'Lovely' are perfect buttons
jokes.explanation: A perfectly escalating list using 'screwing' as a multi-valent word that shifts from political to sexual to literary to broadcast. The structure is impeccable: each beat escalates the betrayal and the wordplay. Tom's helpful addition of 'Also discussed it on television' (he volunteered this himself) and Selina's dry 'Lovely' are the perfect button. One of the episode's finest extended exchanges.

jokes.text: Jonah is caught on tape saying 'retarded' again immediately after his apology — 'What is wrong with you? Are you fuckin' retarded?!'
jokes.setup: Jonah has just delivered a formal apology (on camera) for saying 'retarded'
jokes.punchline: He immediately uses the word again — mid-apology tour — having forgotten the word he was apologizing for
jokes.explanation: The structure is perfect: an entire arc of sensitivity training, an apology, and then the immediate relapse in the same breath. The payoff of the sensitivity training subplot, delivered with maximum irony. Jonah's complete inability to learn anything even while apologizing for it is character-perfect. The 'I'm so sorry / I accept your apology' beat with the person he insulted is a lovely extra layer.

jokes.text: One voter at a time, Teddy. / But Jonah, there were two of them.
jokes.setup: Jonah walks away from his latest disaster with misplaced confidence
jokes.punchline: His inspirational 'one voter at a time' is immediately undermined by Beth's literal correction that there were actually two people there
jokes.explanation: Good economy and structure. Beth's literal correction deflates Jonah's delusion while also inadvertently defending him (there were TWO voters). The gap between Jonah's self-perception and reality is the consistent engine of his comedy.

jokes.text: Thank you! 'I've had enough, Andrew! Mike McLintock has had enough!'
jokes.setup: Mike is on medication, distracted, and trying to write an email to Andrew about losing his savings
jokes.punchline: A colleague says 'I think you've had enough' about the pills; Mike mishears this as inspiration for his email sign-off, shouting it as a declarative
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — the overheard comment becomes email inspiration. Mike's manic state and the third-person self-reference ('Mike McLintock has had enough!') is excellent. The enthusiasm with which he seizes on this accidental phrase is very Mike.

jokes.text: Novelty mayors are Iowa's number one source of tourism. After tornado-chasing. And coming into town to buy Sudafed.
jokes.setup: Catherine notes the surprisingly large turnout for a dog mayor's funeral
jokes.punchline: Richard's deadpan delivery of the Iowa tourism hierarchy: novelty mayors → tornado-chasing → buying Sudafed (implying meth production)
jokes.explanation: Three-beat escalation with a strong finish. The Sudafed/meth implication is delivered by Richard as a neutral tourism fact, which is perfect — he doesn't understand it's a drug-culture reference. Combines observational state satire with character comedy. 'This isn't Nebraska' is a nice button.

jokes.text: Is that a mayor as well? / That's just a cat. / Could you imagine? / This isn't Nebraska.
jokes.setup: At the dog mayor's funeral, someone spots a cat
jokes.punchline: The deadpan exchange about whether the cat is also a mayor, ending with 'This isn't Nebraska' as if Nebraska's animal mayoralty situation is well-established and worse
jokes.explanation: 'This isn't Nebraska' as a territorial defense of Iowa's animal mayor standards is absurdist geography comedy. Richard's delivery makes it work — he's earnest about the distinction.

jokes.text: Would you like me to neuter the new mayor?
jokes.setup: The judge asks Richard about the search for a successor to the deceased dog mayor
jokes.punchline: Richard offers to neuter the next mayor as a practical service, missing the significance of the question
jokes.explanation: Richard's literal helpfulness in a dog-mayor context is a reliable bit. The offer to neuter the next political leader is a nice absurdist beat.

jokes.text: What the fuck? I gotta debate Dumbledong?
jokes.setup: Jonah encounters another debate candidate in a wizard costume
jokes.punchline: 'Dumbledong' — Jonah's portmanteau of Dumbledore with a phallic suffix, delivered with genuine competitive outrage
jokes.explanation: 'Dumbledong' is a clean portmanteau joke — exactly Jonah's register of insult, reflexively phallic but not without creativity. The competitive indignation makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You have to be more PC than a clit ring made out of wheatgrass.
jokes.setup: Teddy is coaching Jonah to be politically correct before the debate
jokes.punchline: The specific metaphor of 'a clit ring made out of wheatgrass' as the gold standard of progressive political correctness
jokes.explanation: Genuinely original simile. The intersection of body-piercing, anatomy, and wellness culture as a metaphor for progressive PC standards is absurdist but internally logical. The image is specific enough to be surprising. Strong originality justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: Brie Ramachandran-Shulhoff
jokes.setup: The undercard debate moderator introduces herself
jokes.punchline: The hyphenated multicultural last name 'Ramachandran-Shulhoff' is itself a joke — the most elaborately inclusive name possible for a cable news debate host
jokes.explanation: A quick background joke about cable news host naming conventions. Not deep but efficiently deployed.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, you can't say that. / Say what, 'Niger'? / It doesn't matter how you pronounce it, we now know that it is wrong.
jokes.setup: The moderator asks about American policy toward Niger (the African nation)
jokes.punchline: Jonah mishears 'Niger' as a racial slur, panics, then saying the name itself multiple times while protesting he shouldn't say it
jokes.explanation: The Niger/slur confusion is a politically precise joke: the sensitivity training has backfired by making Jonah hyperaware of sound-alikes. The structure is excellent — each attempt to clarify repeats the offense. 'Only he can say that' implies Jonah is deferring to the Black wizard-costumed candidate. The callback to the sensitivity training gives it extra punch. The whole sequence escalates beautifully.

jokes.text: Jesus, Clark, you're standing right next to the guy. / What did he say? / No, he said it, not me. / But for the record, I think that the United States should send troops into countries like N-word, and I think it would be best if those troops were black.
jokes.setup: The Niger debate moment is already chaotic
jokes.punchline: Jonah pivots to saying 'N-word' as if it's the same, then doubles down by specifying Black troops — his solution to his racism being additionally racist
jokes.explanation: Escalation of the Niger joke — Jonah's solution to saying the country name is to say 'N-word' instead (still saying the sound), then his racial troop policy is a perfect character extension. The logic is Jonah's own: solving racism with more racism. Highly character-integrated and structurally excellent.

jokes.text: Can I just say, making friends overseas is powerful magic. / You ask him any question, it's gonna be 'powerful magic.' / I'm gonna ask him a question. What's your solution to fix our failing public education system? / Well, longer school years, free breakfast... and magic!
jokes.setup: The wizard candidate gives a debate answer about foreign policy
jokes.punchline: His answer is 'powerful magic'; a staffer bets that any question will get the same answer; the bet is proven right about education too — 'and magic!'
jokes.explanation: Beautiful call-and-response escalation. The setup/prediction/payoff is textbook structure. 'And magic!' as the education policy answer — with 'longer school years, free breakfast' as reasonable-sounding preamble — is well-built. The staffer's correct prediction makes the audience feel clever for expecting it.

jokes.text: Little Richard will be very happy for his daddy-- the new mayor of Lurlene. / Catherine, he won't be making memories for another 22 months.
jokes.setup: Catherine is excited to tell her baby about Richard becoming mayor
jokes.punchline: Marjorie's correction that the baby won't form memories for 22 months — literal, robotic, and exactly right
jokes.explanation: Marjorie's character is entirely built on literal precision replacing emotional warmth. '22 months' is specific enough to feel researched, which is perfect for Marjorie. Classic deadpan character joke.

jokes.text: Oh, look at this. Congressman Ryan! And this must be Mrs. Ryan. Or do you go by your maiden name: Mrs. Ryan? / Oh, either is fine. / No, ma'am, she's my half-sister. / Step. / Gary: She's his step-sister.
jokes.setup: Selina meets Jonah's wife Beth for the first time
jokes.punchline: Selina's maiden name joke lands wrong when Jonah clarifies Beth is his half-sister (then step-sister with corrections) — the relationship is already deeply weird; Selina's joke inadvertently exposes it
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe structure — Selina makes a routine quip that accidentally reveals something genuinely uncomfortable. The half/step-sister clarification argument happening in front of her deepens the discomfort. Multiple parties are embarrassed simultaneously. Gary's helpful clarification as final confirmation is a perfect button.

jokes.text: It seems I have been appointed the new Mayor of Lurlene. / Oh, congratulations! / Mayor Splizzle in the hizzle! / Wow. All right. / But don't you have to be a dog? / Well, legally, yes, but it's unenforceable.
jokes.setup: Richard announces he's been appointed to replace the deceased dog mayor
jokes.punchline: The legal requirement to be a dog being dismissed as 'unenforceable' — delivered by Richard as a simple legal technicality
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the dog mayor thread. 'Legally, yes, but it's unenforceable' as a response to the requirement to be a dog is absurdist gold. Richard's complete comfort with this situation, plus Jonah's 'Mayor Splizzle in the hizzle' and Selina's weary 'Wow. All right.' make this a multi-character comedic beat.

jokes.text: What?! Mike's entire life savings? So we're talking about, what, $6?
jokes.setup: Selina learns Mike published a piece accusing Andrew of stealing his life savings
jokes.punchline: Selina reduces Mike's life savings to $6 — a perfect encapsulation of her contempt for his financial station
jokes.explanation: The specific '$6' amount is the right kind of precision — contemptuous and specific. Selina's class contempt for Mike is consistently well-deployed, and this is an efficient delivery of it.

jokes.text: The term 'Fonzie scheme' is referenced. And the article as a whole is not cool.
jokes.setup: Kent is summarizing Mike's BuzzFeed article about Andrew's fraud
jokes.punchline: 'Fonzie scheme' as Mike's malapropism for 'Ponzi scheme,' followed by Kent's double meaning: 'not cool' refers both to unfavorable and to Fonzie's iconic coolness
jokes.explanation: Double-layered joke of exceptional economy. Mike's 'Fonzie scheme' malapropism for 'Ponzi scheme' is the setup; Kent's 'not cool' is a pun on both the Fonzie/cool connection and the standard meaning. Only Kent would deliver this with zero acknowledgment of the pun. Exceptional word economy — the joke does enormous work in very few words. Justifies 8.5 craft.

jokes.text: Get that Benedict Ronald McDonald on the phone right now.
jokes.setup: Selina is furious at Mike for the BuzzFeed article
jokes.punchline: 'Benedict Ronald McDonald' — combining Benedict Arnold (traitor) with Ronald McDonald (clown/idiot) into a single compound insult for Mike
jokes.explanation: Selina's compound proper-noun insults are a signature of the show, and this is one of the better ones. The fusion of 'traitor' (Benedict Arnold) and 'clown' (Ronald McDonald) perfectly captures both the betrayal and the contempt. Economical and memorable.

jokes.text: Fat Guy's phone. / Selina: Mike, what the backstabbing fuck?! / Dude, your mom's on the phone. She sounds pissed.
jokes.setup: Selina calls Mike's cell phone in a rage
jokes.punchline: Someone at BuzzFeed answers (calling him 'Fat Guy'), and then dismisses the call as his mom
jokes.explanation: Nice structural joke — Selina's presidential fury is diminished to 'your mom's on the phone.' The callback to 'Fat Guy' adds texture. The trivialization of Selina's authority is consistently funny.

jokes.text: Did you know that President Meyer had a heart attack a couple of years ago? Just covered it up! — Well, you had a heart attack. — This was a minor prostate procedure.
jokes.setup: Tom is lobbing medical revelations about Selina in the debate
jokes.punchline: Selina corrects 'heart attack' to 'minor prostate procedure' — an organ she does not have — suggesting even her medical cover-up has a cover-up
jokes.explanation: Selina insisting it was a 'prostate procedure' — an anatomical impossibility for her — is a perfect character joke. Her cover story is so committed that it overrides basic anatomy. The confidence with which she corrects the record on her own impossible procedure is characteristic Selina self-delusion.

jokes.text: Olu-wakemi Talibah Talbot. — Oh. — That sounds like a terrorist to me, folks. — Talibah, Taliban. — Taliban. That's an old chestnut.
jokes.setup: Selina is looking for an attack on Kemi during debate intermission
jokes.punchline: Selina floats the 'Talibah sounds like Taliban' xenophobic attack; Ben deflates it with 'That's an old chestnut' — acknowledging it as a tired, well-worn playbook move
jokes.explanation: 'That's an old chestnut' as the dismissal of a racist attack is sharp — not because it's morally wrong, but because it's too well-worn to be effective. Ben's strategic cynicism about the political utility of racism is perfectly calibrated Veep satire.

jokes.text: God bless America for hating women almost as much as I do.
jokes.setup: Selina wins the crowd with her 'man up' speech — a feminist-coded message that she delivered while actually hating the message
jokes.punchline: Selina's private admission that she shares America's misogyny — her own feminist victory is powered by internalized sexism
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke and one of the best lines in the entire series. It encapsulates Selina's entire character: she's a woman who built her career on a misogynist system not by fighting it but by absorbing it. Her feminist triumph is powered by anti-feminist feeling. The joke is structurally perfect (the setup is her 'man up' success; the punchline destroys the uplift), character-defining (ONLY Selina says this), deeply satirical (about American politics, gender, and self-deception), and devastatingly economical. Near-maximum scores across the board are fully justified.

jokes.text: Feels like someone shoved a red-hot pair of barbecue tongs up my joy trail.
jokes.setup: Dan asks how Amy is feeling post-procedure
jokes.punchline: 'Red-hot pair of barbecue tongs up my joy trail' — an inventive and grotesque description using cookout equipment and a euphemism for vagina
jokes.explanation: 'Joy trail' as a euphemism is a delightful coinage; 'barbecue tongs' is a specific, vivid, unexpected choice. Amy's style of deadpan self-description using violent and grotesque imagery is perfectly character-integrated. This line deserves to be remembered. High craft scores are justified.

jokes.text: Frozen maxi-pads soaked in comfrey extract. My own personal recipe. / That is so nice. / And also... cold and disgusting.
jokes.setup: Dan has brought something for post-procedure comfort
jokes.punchline: The reveal of comfrey-extract maxi-pads as Dan's 'personal recipe' is both unexpectedly caring and grotesque; Amy's reaction — 'so nice' then 'cold and disgusting' — perfectly balances the tonal dissonance
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Dan's gesture is simultaneously tender and horrifying. 'My own personal recipe' implies he has made these before — raising many questions. Amy's two-beat reaction ('so nice' / 'cold and disgusting') is perfect tonal whiplash. Character-integrated: only these two characters in this dynamic.

jokes.text: I'm gonna need that back when you're done, 'cause Leon will pay big bucks for it.
jokes.setup: Dan gives Amy the comfrey maxi-pad
jokes.punchline: Dan intends to sell it to a journalist (Leon) — treating a medical item as a tabloid commodity
jokes.explanation: The kicker to the maxi-pad bit — Dan's 'kindness' is revealed as opportunism. Only Dan would have this plan. The specificity of 'Leon' (a press contact) grounds it in Veep's world. Character-perfect punchline that retroactively reframes his gesture.

jokes.text: See? When you do good things, good things happen to good people. / I'm such a good person.
jokes.setup: Amy has just accepted Teddy's offer to join Jonah's campaign
jokes.punchline: Amy's cheerful self-affirmation immediately after being recruited for a disastrous campaign, post-abortion, with the maxi-pad situation still ongoing
jokes.explanation: Amy's declaration that she is 'a good person' coming directly after an abortion and accepting employment on Jonah's catastrophic campaign is pure Veep irony. The disconnect between her self-image and reality is the joke. Character-specific delivery makes it land.

jokes.text: Can you make me a waxing appointment? I'm feeling patriotic. Full bald eagle.
jokes.setup: Selina is celebrating her debate success
jokes.punchline: 'Full bald eagle' as a request for a full Brazilian wax — the patriotic bird as a grooming term — delivered with victory energy
jokes.explanation: 'Full bald eagle' is a genuinely original coinage for a Brazilian wax that integrates national symbolism with personal grooming. The patriotic framing of a beauty appointment captures Selina's narcissism — winning a debate is personal enough to justify grooming. Economical and memorable.

jokes.text: I just found out from my stupid stepfather-- / Father-in-law! / From my stupid stepfather-in-law, that math was created by Muslims.
jokes.setup: Jonah is talking about non-college educated white voters
jokes.punchline: The reveal that math being 'created by Muslims' is Jonah's new piece of information — and his stepfather-in-law is the source — combines anti-intellectualism with barely-veiled Islamophobia
jokes.explanation: Historically, algebra was developed in the Islamic world — so Jonah is accidentally correct while meaning it as a smear. The fact that he learned this from his stepfather-in-law adds character texture. The 'Father-in-law!' correction from Teddy (the step/father running gag) adds a layer.

jokes.text: I would really love to be able to count on your endorsement. — You gonna make me say it? We got race in the race.
jokes.setup: Selina is seeking an endorsement from a Southern politician post-debate
jokes.punchline: 'We got race in the race' — the politician's blunt acknowledgment that racial politics are the primary driver of the endorsement decision
jokes.explanation: 'We got race in the race' is a great ending line — both a political observation and a pun (race/race). The bluntness of the Southern politician acknowledging racial calculus with zero embarrassment is sharp satire. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: I would really love to be able to count on your endorsement. / You gonna make me say it? We got race in the race.
jokes.setup: Selina seeks an endorsement from a Southern politician in South Carolina
jokes.punchline: 'We got race in the race' — the politician's frank admission that Kemi's race is a political factor, which is simultaneously an endorsement of racism and an honest political assessment
jokes.explanation: 'We got race in the race' is a strong turn of phrase — compact, dark, and pointed political satire. The episode ends on this note, implying Selina will use racism as a campaign tool. Economical and resonant.

tmdb_overview: In the run-up to the first debate, Selina makes a novel campaign proposal. Comments by Jonah create blowback. Dan and Amy take a road trip.
insight: Political desperation hits peak absurdity as 71 rapid-fire jokes sustain Veep's endgame chaos.
